context: Samuel was sent to that place in order to anoint the one that would be the next king of Israel...
point_summary: God's sovereign selection of David—overlooked and uncalled by his own family, yet chosen by divine appointment to shepherd Israel—illustrates that God's purposes for a person's life are not determined by human estimation.

context: I preached the message there in 1 Samuel, with Samuel, where he says, speak Lord, for thy servant heareth.
point_summary: God answers those who seek Him, as Rebekah's inquiry of the Lord parallels Samuel's posture of listening — a reminder that divine revelation comes to those who ask.

context: There's but a step between me and death and as I've often thought about that passage of Scripture The reality in David's life is that yes Saul was hunting him
point_summary: David's words in 1 Samuel 20:3 — "there is but a step between me and death" — are applied to underscore that death's nearness is universal, not merely a circumstance of David's flight from Saul.

context: Samuel said, half the Lord is great delight in burnt offerings and sacrifices as in obeying the voice of the Lord. And then it says this, behold, to obey is better than sacrifice and to hearken than the fat of rams.
point_summary: Obedience to God's voice surpasses religious ritual, as Samuel's declaration to Saul establishes that straightforward divine instruction leaves no room for substituting sacrifice in place of compliance.

context: Samuel makes it abundantly clear in the first opening verses of 1 Samuel 15 that this instruction is from God Almighty.
point_summary: Chapter and context introduction; the sermon notes that Samuel's opening words in verse 1 make unmistakably clear that Saul's instructions came from God Almighty, not from Samuel's own authority.

context: Saul gathered the people together in verse number four, numbered them in Telam, 200,000 foot men and 10,000 men of Judah.
point_summary: Saul's mustering of 200,000 footmen and 10,000 men of Judah demonstrates he received God's command clearly and had the resources to obey it fully, making his subsequent disobedience without excuse.

context: Saul then comes to the city of Amalek and laid weight in the valley there in verse number five.
point_summary: Saul's arrival at the city of Amalek in verse 5 marks the point where God's clear, concise command was received but ultimately not fully obeyed.

context: Notice in verse number seven, it says that Saul smote the Amalekites from Havilah until thou comest to Shur.
point_summary: Despite receiving clear and concise divine instruction to destroy Amalek utterly, Saul selectively obeyed—sparing Agag and the best livestock—revealing that partial obedience is disobedience.

context: he took Agag, the king of the Amalekites, alive and utterly destroyed all the people at the edge of the sword.
point_summary: Saul's partial obedience — sparing Agag and the best livestock while destroying only what was worthless — constituted a failure to perform God's commandments, not a fulfillment of them.

context: But Saul and the people spared Agag and the best of the sheep and of the oxen and of the fatlings and the lambs and all that was good and would not utterly destroy them.
point_summary: Saul's partial obedience—sparing Agag and the best livestock while destroying only what was worthless—exposes that selective compliance with God's command is still disobedience.

context: But everything that was vile and refuse that they destroyed utterly. Now, Saul isn't going to get away with it, is he? Samuel's not there, but the word of the Lord came unto Samuel in verse number 10.
point_summary: God's direct declaration — "it repenteth me that I have set up Saul to be king" — reveals that Saul's failure to perform God's commandments carried immediate divine consequence, even before Samuel arrived on the scene.

context: And notice what God says, it repenteth me that I have set up Saul to be king, for he has turned back from following me and hath not performed my commandments. Hath not performed my commandments. He failed to obey. I want you to notice as a side note this evening, at the end of verse number 11, it says, and it grieved Samuel and he cried unto the Lord all night.
point_summary: Saul's disobedience grieved Samuel so deeply that he cried to God all night, revealing how seriously a faithful spiritual leader takes his God-given responsibility to those he is called to serve.

context: Samuel came to Saul and Saul said unto him, blessed be thou of the Lord. And then notice what he says, I have performed the commandment of the Lord.
point_summary: Saul's confident claim to have obeyed while the sheep still bleat exposes the self-deception at the heart of partial obedience, which the sermon treats as disobedience in full.

context: Samuel says to Saul in verse 16, stay and I will tell thee what the Lord hath said to me this night.
point_summary: Samuel's confrontation of Saul—"stay, and I will tell thee what the Lord hath said"—frames the exchange as a divine verdict on Saul's self-deception, exposing partial obedience as disobedience and evil in God's sight.

context: And Saul said unto Samuel, yea, I have obeyed the voice of the Lord and have gone the way which the Lord sent me and have brought Agath. the king of Amalek, and have utterly destroyed the Amalekites.
point_summary: Saul's self-deceived claim of full obedience, followed by blame-shifting to the people, illustrates that partial obedience is disobedience and can manifest as self-justification before God.

context: but the people, in verse number 21, took of the spoil sheep and oxen, the chief of the things, which should have been utterly destroyed, to sacrifice unto the Lord thy God in Gilgal.
point_summary: Saul's self-justifying claim in verse 21 — blaming the people for sparing the spoil — illustrates how disobedience is compounded by a shift of blame rather than honest confession.

context: Samuel, we read it by way of introduction in verse number 22, Samuel speaks to Saul
point_summary: Partial obedience is disobedience — Saul's self-justification, blame-shifting, and selective compliance with God's command illustrate why Samuel's rebuke in verse 22 stands as an enduring principle for God's people.

context: behold, to obey is better than sacrifice and to hearken than the fat of rams
point_summary: God's own institution of sacrificial worship makes Saul's defense—sparing animals to sacrifice them—all the more inexcusable, since God declares obedience to His voice a greater delight than the offerings He Himself ordained.

context: and I begin to think about this text of Scripture in 1 Samuel 15, when God uses Samuel to confront Saul about his disobedience,
point_summary: Creation, demons, wind, and waves all obey God instinctively, making Saul's willful disobedience in 1 Samuel 15 all the more inexcusable and obedience all the more critical.

context: and he says to obey is better.
point_summary: Samuel's rebuke of Saul establishes that obedience to God is not optional but critical, because God is trustworthy and consistently blesses those who walk in obedience to him.

context: Saul had a choice in 1 Samuel 15. Did he not? He had a choice. It was not that the instruction wasn't clear.
point_summary: Saul's disobedience in 1 Samuel 15 is framed as a deliberate choice, not a failure of clarity—he understood God's command but substituted his own reasoning and the people's preferences for it.

context: Not taking the approach of Saul in 1 Samuel 15, and excusing our disobedience, justifying our partial obedience.
point_summary: Saul's example in 1 Samuel 15 warns against excusing or partially obeying God's clear commands, since God's word remains authoritative and unchanged regardless of shifting circumstances.

context: I mentioned this morning in 1 Samuel chapter number 16 and how that unnamed servant of Saul, at the end of his description of how he's a cunning player of the harp and all that he says about David, he doesn't even use his name in the passage
point_summary: The unnamed servant's description of David in 1 Samuel 16 illustrates that God's presence with a believer should be visibly evident to outside observers, even those who do not know the person's name.

context: Samuel said, When thou wast little in thine own sight, dost thou not make the head of the tribes of Israel, and the Lord anointed the king over Israel?
point_summary: Saul's downfall is traced to the moment he ceased to be "little in his own sight," replacing humble dependence on God's explicit command with self-sufficient judgment.

context: Samuel was very clear, I'll mention this this evening, he was very clear in verses 1 and 2 that this was the commandment of the Lord.
point_summary: Saul's failure is traced to pride displacing humility: when he ceased to be "little in his own sight," he disobeyed God's clear commandment delivered through Samuel in that chapter.

context: Samuel was very clear, I'll mention this this evening, he was very clear in verses 1 and 2 that this was the commandment of the Lord.
point_summary: Saul's failure to obey God's clear command in 1 Samuel 15:1–2 is cited as evidence that pride had displaced the humility—being "little in his own sight"—that once marked his obedience.

context: Samuel also said unto Saul, the Lord sent me to anoint thee to be king over his people, over Israel. Now therefore hearken thou unto the voice of the words of the Lord.
point_summary: God's command to destroy Amalek in 1 Samuel 15:1–3 is grounded in divine memory of Amalek's prior aggression against Israel, and cannot be rightly understood apart from that historical context in Exodus.

context: So when read first Samuel 15 apart from Exodus 17 apart from Deuteronomy 25
point_summary: 1 Samuel 15 must be read alongside Exodus 17 and Deuteronomy 25 to understand God's commanded destruction of Amalek as the fulfillment of a long-established divine judgment, not arbitrary cruelty.

context: Samuel speaking to Saul in 1st Samuel 15, to obey is better than sacrifice.
point_summary: Partial obedience, illustrated by Saul's failure to fully destroy the Amalekites, is treated as disobedience, because God places a premium on complete obedience to His prescribed commands.

context: 'Only fear the Lord and serve Him in truth with all your heart for consider how great things He hath done for you.'
point_summary: Fearing and serving God wholeheartedly is grounded in remembrance of what He has done, cited here as one of a chain of Scripture passages calling for whole-hearted devotion.

context: we're going to pick it up in 1 Samuel chapter 24
point_summary: Recognizing God's providence enables restraint over the flesh's impulse to retaliate, and David's life in 1 Samuel 24 illustrates the spiritual maturity of trusting God rather than taking matters into one's own hands.

context: where he was in 1 Samuel chapter number 16
point_summary: Recognizing God's providence enables restraint; David's anointing in 1 Samuel 16—while faithfully tending sheep, uninvited, yet chosen—illustrates that spiritual maturity trusts God's control rather than forcing outcomes.

context: David is anointed to be king of Israel in 1 Samuel chapter 16.
point_summary: David's anointing as king in 1 Samuel 16 illustrates that God sovereignly chooses and positions His servants without their seeking it, calling believers to trust His control rather than take matters into their own hands.

context: And in chapter number 17, we read about Goliath and all that takes place...
point_summary: David's defeat of Goliath in 1 Samuel 17 is cited as evidence that he acted in obedience to his father's errand, not personal ambition, trusting God rather than taking matters into his own hands.

context: And we come to 1 Samuel chapter 24, and several other things have transpired, but I want you to see in chapter 24 and verse number 4, the Bible says that the men of David said unto him, behold the day of which the Lord said unto thee, they're now in the cave there, and Saul's there.
point_summary: Even when his own men urged him to seize the moment as God-given, David refused to harm Saul because he recognized Saul as the Lord's anointed, and his conscience smote him for even cutting his robe.

context: He had taken these 3,000 chosen men, according to verse number 2, to seek David.
point_summary: Saul's pursuit of David with 3,000 chosen men establishes the life-threatening pressure under which David refused to take vengeance into his own hands against God's anointed.

context: the day of which the Lord said unto thee, behold, I will deliver thine enemy into thine hand that thou mayest do to him as it shall seem good unto thee. Then David arose and cut off the skirt of Saul's robe privily.
point_summary: Even when God's providence places an enemy within reach and companions urge action, restraint toward the Lord's anointed—not retaliation—is the right response.

context: And it came to pass afterward that David's heart smote him because he had cut off Saul's skirt.
point_summary: Even when provoked by his own men to act, David's conscience smote him for the smallest offense against God's anointed, refusing to stretch forth his hand against Saul.

context: And he said unto his men, the Lord forbid that I should do this thing unto my master, the Lord's anointed. Notice what he says, to stretch forth mine hand against him, seeing he is the anointed of the Lord.
point_summary: Restraint toward God's anointed—not retaliation—marks David's response when his own men urge him to seize the moment and kill Saul.

context: So David stayed his servants with these words and suffered them not to rise against Saul. But Saul rose up out of the cave and went on his way.
point_summary: Resisting the counsel of others who urge retaliation, David refused to strike Saul because he recognized him as the Lord's anointed and would not take matters into his own hands.

context: David also arose afterward and went out of the cave and cried after Saul, saying, My Lord, the king. When Saul looked behind him, David stooped with his face to the earth and bowed himself.
point_summary: Even when others urge retaliation and opportunity presents itself, David refused to strike God's anointed, demonstrating that restraint—not revenge—marks the man who trusts God's timing.

context: And verse 9 says that David said to Saul, Wherefore hearest thou men's words, saying, Behold, David seeketh thy hurt.
point_summary: Even when others urge retaliation, David's refusal to harm Saul despite having opportunity demonstrates that godly restraint resists the pressure of those who provoke us to take matters into our own hands.

context: Ziphites came unto Saul in verse number 1 to Gibeah, saying, Doth not David hide himself in the hill of Hacaliah, which is before Jeshimon?
point_summary: 1 Samuel 26:1 sets the scene of Saul's renewed pursuit of David in the wilderness of Ziph, framing the second opportunity David had to take vengeance into his own hands but chose restraint instead.

context: Notice in verse number 3, it says that Saul pitched in the hill of Hacaliah, which is before Jeshimon, by the way.
point_summary: The wilderness setting of 1 Samuel 26:3 establishes the moment when David, surrounded and pursued by Saul's three thousand men, chose restraint over retaliation despite having every circumstantial opportunity to act otherwise.

context: look down, if you will, to verse number 7. The Bible says, So David and Abishai came to the people by night, and behold, Saul lay sleeping within the trench, and his spear stuck in the ground at his bolster.
point_summary: Even when circumstances and companions present a seemingly God-given opportunity for vengeance, restraint is required because acting against the Lord's anointed brings guilt.

context: David said to Abishai, destroy him not. For who can stretch forth his hand against the Lord's anointed and be guiltless?
point_summary: Vengeance against God's anointed belongs to God alone, and David refuses to let even a willing surrogate act in his place against Saul.

context: David said furthermore, as the Lord liveth, the Lord shall smite him. Or his day shall come to die, or he shall descend into battle and perish.
point_summary: David's refusal to let Abishai strike Saul rests on his conviction that God is alive and in control, and that vengeance against the Lord's anointed belongs to the Lord alone.

context: The Lord forbid that I should stretch forth mine hand against the Lord's anointed, but I pray thee, take thou now the spear that is in his bolster and the crews of water, and let us go.
point_summary: David's refusal to let Abishai strike Saul illustrates that trusting God as the living Lord means leaving vengeance in His hands rather than acting through a willing proxy.

context: So David took the spear and the crews of water from Saul's bolster, and they got them away. No man saw it, nor knew it, neither awaked. For they were all asleep, because a deep sleep from the Lord was fallen upon them.
point_summary: God's providential sleep falling on Saul's camp confirms David's conviction that the living Lord—not human hands—would deal with His anointed in His own time.

context: David said, I'm not going to lift my hand against the Lord's anointed, but Abishai, I'm not going to allow you to either. He forbid him. He forbade him.
point_summary: David's refusal to let Abishai strike Saul rests on the conviction that the living God will deal with His anointed in His own time, making human vengeance both unnecessary and guilty.

context: Interference between these two events in the life of David is 1 Samuel chapter 25. And in 1 Samuel 25, I believe that God records for us a significant interaction between David and Abigail that helps us with understanding this whole process of not taking matters into our own hands.
point_summary: Positioned between two accounts of David resisting vengeance against Saul, 1 Samuel 25 is identified as a key passage illustrating the principle of not taking matters into one's own hands.

context: We find that David, in 1 Samuel 24, God gives an opportunity when Saul enters into the cave and David says, no, I'm going to resist that.
point_summary: When God provides an opportunity for revenge, trusting Him means refusing to act—as David twice declined to strike Saul, choosing instead to let the Lord deal with His anointed.

context: In 1 Samuel chapter 26, God gives another opportunity and Abishai says, let me just smite him once. David says, no, I'm going to trust the Lord.
point_summary: When God provides a second opportunity for David to kill Saul, David again refuses, restraining even Abishai, trusting that the Lord himself will deal with the anointed king.

context: But in 1 Samuel 25, there's a different event that takes place that's not with Saul.
point_summary: Positioned between two episodes of David refusing to harm Saul, 1 Samuel 25 illustrates the temptation to take matters into one's own hands against an adversary who is not the Lord's anointed.

context: Notice David says in verse number 21 of 1 Samuel 25. Now David had said, surely in vain have I kept all this fellow hath in the wilderness so that nothing was missed of all that pertained unto him. And he hath requited me evil for good.
point_summary: When good deeds are repaid with evil, the temptation to take violent revenge into one's own hands—as David vowed against Nabal—mirrors the same failure seen in Dinah's brothers.

context: He says in verse 22, so and more also do God unto the enemies of David. If I leave of all that pertain to him by the morning light.
point_summary: When wronged by Nabal's ingratitude, David resolved to take lethal vengeance into his own hands—illustrating that even godly men can fall into the same error as Jacob's sons when they act on wrath rather than waiting on God.

context: And when David heard that Nabal was dead, he said, Blessed be the Lord. Notice what he says, That hath pleaded the cause of my reproach from the hand of Nabal, and hath kept his servant from evil. For the Lord hath returned the wickedness of Nabal upon his own head.
point_summary: When God intervenes to judge the wicked, the one who waited rather than acting in vengeance is kept from evil and bears no reproach on his testimony.

context: after all that we considered from 1 Samuel 25
point_summary: Sandwiched between two episodes involving Saul, David's near-vengeance against Nabal illustrates how taking matters into one's own hands reflects a failure to trust that God will plead one's cause and avenge wrongs himself.

context: And churches all across this land have Ichabod written over them, the glory of the Lord has departed.
point_summary: When churches drift leftward with the culture and abandon biblical standards, they become like Ichabod — the glory of the Lord has departed.

context: Moreover, ask for me, God forbid, that I should sin against the Lord in ceasing to pray for you.
point_summary: Ceasing to pray for others is itself a sin against God, as Samuel's own confession frames prayerlessness not merely as neglect but as moral failure before the Lord.

context: The Bible begins in verse number one and says then they told David saying behold the Philistines fight against Keilah
point_summary: David's inquiry of the Lord and subsequent deliverance of Keilah from the Philistines serves as the opening context for the chapter's broader lesson, introduced here as background before the sermon's focus on verses 16–18.

context: And it was told Saul that David was come to Keilah. And Saul said, God hath delivered him into mine hand; for he is shut in, by entering into a town that hath gates and bars.
point_summary: Saul misreads David's presence in the walled city of Keilah as a divine trap, declaring "God hath delivered him into mine hand," revealing how victory can immediately give way to new danger.

context: And Saul called all the people together to war, to go down to Keilah, to besiege David and his men.
point_summary: Saul's summoning of his forces to besiege Keilah contrasts sharply with David's prior deliverance of that same city, exposing Saul's pursuit of David as self-serving rather than protective of God's people.

context: early on in 1st Samuel chapter number 18, in fact when Saul has this idea that he was going to send David out to war
point_summary: Jonathan's awareness of Saul's secret murderous agenda toward David—evident from 1 Samuel 18's account of Saul's schemes—forms the backdrop for understanding why his encouragement of David was so deliberate and costly.

context: and it came to pass when he had made an end of speaking unto Saul That the soul of Jonathan was knit with the soul of David Jonathan loved him as his own soul
point_summary: Jonathan's deep covenant love for David—soul knit to soul and sealed by the giving of his robe, sword, and bow—established the friendship that made him both aware of and positioned to counter Saul's secret agenda against David.

context: then Jonathan and David made a covenant because he loved him as his own soul
point_summary: The covenant Jonathan made with David — rooted in a love as deep as his own soul — established the friendship that made Jonathan aware of and responsive to Saul's murderous agenda against David.

context: And Jonathan stripped himself of the robe that was upon him and gave it to David and his garments even to a sword
point_summary: Jonathan's covenant with David in 1 Samuel 18:1–4, marked by the giving of his robe and weapons, established the deep friendship that made Jonathan fully aware of — and opposed to — his father's secret agenda to destroy David.

context: Saul spake to Jonathan his son and to all his servants notice this that they should kill David
point_summary: Saul's previously secret agenda to kill David becomes an open command in 19:1, marking the moment Jonathan is forced to choose between loyalty to his father and his covenant friendship with David.

context: for he did put his life in his hand and slew the Philistine and the Lord brought a great salvation for all Israel thou sawest it and did rejoice
point_summary: Jonathan's appeal to Saul against killing David rests on David's innocent blood and God-given deliverance of Israel, framing the proposed murder as sin without cause.

context: David returns to Ziklag and finds the Amalekites had taken captives
point_summary: In contrast to 1 Samuel 30, where David had no Jonathan and encouraged himself in the Lord alone, here in chapter 23 God sent Jonathan to physically strengthen David's hand in God during his wilderness flight from Saul.

context: Jonathan strengthens David's hand in God
point_summary: In 1 Samuel 23, Jonathan's deliberate journey to find David in the wilderness illustrates how God uses faithful friends to strengthen and encourage his people in their most desperate moments.

context: Relationship between Jonathan and David
point_summary: The covenant friendship between Jonathan and David, traced from 1 Samuel 18 through 20, provides the relational foundation that makes Jonathan's arrival to strengthen David in the wilderness so meaningful.

context: Relationship between Jonathan and David
point_summary: Jonathan's willingness to leave safety and go to David in the wilderness illustrates how God uses covenant friendship to strengthen and encourage his people in their most desperate moments.

context: ...And the Lord said unto Samuel, How long wilt thou mourn for Saul? Seeing I have rejected him from reigning over Israel. Fill thine horn with oil, and go, I will send thee to Jesse the Bethlehemite, for I have provided me a king among his sons.
point_summary: God's rebuke of Samuel's prolonged grief over Saul — "How long wilt thou mourn?" — frames the divine appointment of David as king, showing that God's sovereign plan moves forward even when his servants linger in sorrow.

context: there's but a step between me and death. And we think about understanding that's there in 1 Samuel 20 in v. 3, the latter part of the verse, but truly as the Lord liveth and as thy soul liveth, there's but a step between me and death.
point_summary: David's confession that "there is but a step between me and death" illustrates the extreme danger he faced as a fugitive, providing the desperate context into which Jonathan spoke God's promises of protection and future kingship.

context: If you read the end of 1 Samuel 23, you find that just as David and his men are completely surrounded by Saul and his men, that the Bible tells us that there came a messenger to Saul and said the Philistines have invaded the land
point_summary: Jonathan's timely visit to strengthen David's hand in God (1 Sam. 23) illustrates that God uses willing, sensitive believers to encourage others in the midst of trials, even as He works miraculously to deliver them.

context: David said in the book of 1 Samuel, there's but a step between me and death
point_summary: David's words in 1 Samuel 20:3—"there is but a step between me and death"—are applied beyond their original context of Saul's pursuit to underscore the universal nearness of death for every person.

context: when he stands before Saul in 1 Samuel chapter number 17, he gives glory to God for giving that victory and that deliverance
point_summary: David's testimony before Saul in 1 Samuel 17 is cited as his own acknowledgment that God—not his personal strength—gave him victory over the bear and lion that threatened his flock.

context: God had anointed through Samuel, David to be the next king of Israel.
point_summary: Referenced as background context establishing that David had been anointed king in chapter 16, setting the narrative foundation before the sermon's focus on David's wise behavior in chapter 18.

context: God provides us with the record of David and Goliath.
point_summary: David's wise behavior—highlighted across 1 Samuel 17–18 as the backdrop to his conduct before Saul and Israel—illustrates what it means practically to "walk in wisdom" as commanded in Colossians 4:5.

context: we begin to read some things about the behavior of David
point_summary: David's wise behavior in 1 Samuel 18—accepted by the people and accompanied by God's presence—illustrates the call to "walk in wisdom" as a pattern of conduct that shapes one's testimony before others.

context: and David went out whithersoever Saul sent him and behaved himself wisely. And Saul set him over the men of war and he was accepted in the sight of all the people and also in the sight of Saul's servants.
point_summary: David's consistent wise behavior—wherever Saul sent him—is presented as a key factor explaining why the Lord was with him, illustrating the connection between walking wisely and God's blessing.

context: and David behaved himself wisely in all his ways and the Lord was with him.
point_summary: David's consistent wise behavior in all his ways is presented as a key factor explaining why the Lord was with him — a pattern the sermon connects to the call for New Testament believers to walk wisely.

context: Notice how the passage of Scripture ends in 1 Samuel 18 and verse number 30. Then the princes of the Philistines went forth and it came to pass after they went forth that David behaved himself more wisely than all the servants of Saul so that his name was much set by.
point_summary: Wise behavior, demonstrated consistently even under pressure, results in a name held in high regard — illustrating the Colossians 4:5 call to walk in wisdom toward outsiders.

context: if you jot down in your notes, first Samuel 16 and verse number 18, it's a powerful verse of scripture
point_summary: David's unnamed observer in 1 Samuel 16:18 illustrates that people are watching believers and forming assessments of their character, prompting the question of what others would say about you.

context: First Samuel 16 and verse number 18, that there's an unnamed servant of Saul that observes David and gives this great recommendation of David being the one that is selected to come into the house of Saul and to play with that evil spirit that troubled him.
point_summary: An unnamed servant's unsolicited commendation of David illustrates that others are constantly observing our lives and forming conclusions about our character, even without our awareness.

reference_text: First Samuel three in verse number ten
context: ...Samuel obeys the instruction that Eli provided. And he said, speak for thy servant hereth, according to First Samuel three in verse number ten...
point_summary: God's direct speech to Samuel in 1 Samuel 3:10 is cited as a biblical example of pre-canon revelation, illustrating that God communicated future events to his people through dreams and visions before Scripture was completed.

reference_text: Speak, Lord, for thy servant heareth
context: Speak, Lord, for thy servant heareth. Every day when I read the Bible, I pray Psalm 119, open thou mine eyes that I may behold wondrous things out of thy law.
point_summary: Approaching God's Word with the posture of "Speak, Lord, for thy servant heareth" is presented as the daily disposition that cultivates readiness for God to move in revival.

context: Samuel 15 and verse number 12. This evening, though the first of this four-part series seems to have a negative note...
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: David prayed likewise in 1 Samuel chapter number 17 when he fights against Goliath
point_summary: David's prayer before fighting Goliath was not for personal fame but that God alone would be exalted—paralleling Hezekiah's petition that all nations would know the Lord.

context: I mentioned this morning about David in 1 Samuel 17. He knew something about God, did he not?
point_summary: David's confidence against Goliath is cited as evidence that experiential knowledge of God's past faithfulness produces active faith in His present ability to act.

context: I do not want, as you would read in the Old Testament, Ichabod written that the glory of the Lord hath departed.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 1 Samuel 17, a familiar passage of Scripture, David fighting Goliath.
point_summary: Those who criticize others for stepping out in faith—like David's brothers attacking him before Goliath—are themselves doing nothing, and will answer to God for it.

context: 1 Samuel 17, is that not what happens?
point_summary: In 1 Samuel 17, David alone acts for God while those who do nothing—Saul, Eliab, and the others—are the very ones casting judgment on him, illustrating that critics should first examine their own hearts.

context: David would say there's but a step between me and death
point_summary: Death's nearness — "there is but a step between me and death" (1 Sam. 20:3) — grounds the call for believers to examine their pilgrimage and live with eternity in view.

context: It's like Samuel, right? When he goes to Eli, you've called. No, I didn't. He comes again, you call, no, I didn't. He says, here's what you do. Next time you hear it, say, speak, Lord, thy servant listens, hears.
point_summary: Whereas God once spoke audibly to his servants, Scripture itself is now his voice, and believers should approach Bible reading and worship with Samuel's posture of expectant, attentive listening.

context: Saul's dealing with an issue where he has an evil spirit that's troubling him. And his counselors say, here's what we've got to do. We've got to find a man that can come in and play, and man, this is going to be great. And they just go, okay, find him. And one of the servants answered and said this in 1 Samuel 16, 18, just jot it down, and we can go back and read it later. He says, behold, I have seen a son of Jesse, the Bethlehemite, that is cunning and playing, and a mighty, valiant man, a man of war, prudent in matters, a comely person, and the Lord is with him.
point_summary: David's life was so consistently godly that a servant who may have been unknown to him could give a thorough, credible testimony of his character, skill, and walk with God.

context: in that great battle against Goliath, and we're familiar with 1 Samuel in chapter number 17, and we look at that passage of Scripture
point_summary: David's refusal of Saul's armor when facing Goliath illustrates that he trusted God alone as deliverer, not his own strength or resources.

context: Ichabod. What's it mean? The glory of the Lord hath departed
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: Speak, Lord, for thy servant heareth
point_summary: Bringing a prepared, receptive heart before God's word is illustrated by Samuel's posture—"Speak, Lord, for thy servant heareth"—held up as the model disposition for daily Bible reading and corporate worship.

context: And God says, no. He's not the one. Don't look on his appearance, or on his countenance. He says that God sees the inward. Man sees the outward, but God sees the inward.
point_summary: God's unseen internal work in the believer is grounded in the truth that, as in Samuel's evaluation of David, man sees only the outward while God sees the inward.
